macro_events.event_summary: SIPRI reports global military spending hit a record $2.9 trillion in 2025, driven by European rearmament and Asian buildup, even as the US pulls back.
primary_drivers: European NATO defense budget expansion driving foreign military sales and local procurement
Relative shift in order book away from US primes toward European primes given US pullback narrative

beneficiaries.rationale: Raytheon is the dominant supplier of Patriot air-defense systems and NASAMS — the exact platforms European nations (Germany, Poland, Romania, Netherlands) are buying in bulk to replenish Ukraine donations and rebuild stockpiles. Direct FMS exposure to European rearmament.

Lockheed sells F-35s to Germany, Poland, Finland, Czech Republic, Romania plus HIMARS and PAC-3 missiles. Largest single beneficiary of European procurement; backlog already at record. Asian buildup (Japan, South Korea F-35 orders) compounds.

General Dynamics' European Land Systems unit (Mowag, Steyr, Santa Bárbara) builds Piranha/ASCOD vehicles bought directly by European militaries; also benefits from artillery shell production ramp via OTS.

Northrop's munitions (solid rocket motors via ATK), IBCS battle-management, and Triton/Global Hawk ISR are pulled by European air-defense integration programs. Less direct than RTX/LMT but clear backlog tailwind.
Leidos' Dynetics builds enduring shield counter-UAS and small glide munitions being procured for Ukraine-style attritable warfare; benefits from global C-UAS spend wave.
Kratos' Valkyrie unmanned systems and target drones are pure-play attritable-systems exposure that scales directly with the kind of asymmetric/drone-heavy procurement Europe and Asia are funding.

Huntington Ingalls is the sole builder of US nuclear carriers and a primary submarine yard — Indo-Pacific buildup and AUKUS sub commitments support multi-year backlog even if domestic US base budget growth slows.

losers.rationale: Boeing Defense is structurally exposed to US DoD programs (KC-46, F-15EX) where the headline flags US pullback; defense unit is also a chronic margin drag, so a relative-rotation-into-defense trade may bypass BA. Weak link, not a strong short.

headlines: Europe’s rearmament push drives global military spending to record $2.9 trillion despite U.S. pullback

macro_events.event_summary: Brent/WTI oil futures spike back above $100/bbl after U.S.-Iran peace talks collapse, raising risk of sanctions enforcement and Middle East supply disruption.
primary_drivers: Higher crude price = direct revenue/margin lift for upstream E&P
Geopolitical risk premium on Middle East supply (Strait of Hormuz, Iran sanctions snapback)
Higher input costs / fuel costs squeeze transports, airlines, and consumer discretionary

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with large upstream exposure; every $10/bbl move in Brent adds several billion in annual operating cash flow. Direct price-taker leverage.
Same upstream torque as XOM; Permian and deepwater production levered directly to crude price. Buyback pace tends to accelerate at >$90 oil.
Pure-play US shale producer (Permian) with high oil cut and operating leverage; one of the highest-beta majors to WTI. Low breakeven means incremental dollars drop to FCF.
Diamondback is a near-pure Permian E&P; oil-weighted production gives near-1:1 cash flow sensitivity to WTI moves above $100.
Oil services demand (completions, frac) reaccelerates at sustained $100 crude as E&Ps add rigs; Halliburton has highest North American shale revenue mix among the big three.
Higher-beta E&P with material Egyptian and North Sea exposure that prices off Brent; also benefits from Middle East geopolitical premium specifically.
Cheniere — collapsed Iran talks raise the probability of tighter sanctions and stronger demand for US LNG cargoes into Europe/Asia as buyers diversify away from Iranian/Russian-linked flows.

losers.rationale: Jet fuel is ~25-30% of airline operating costs and is unhedged or lightly hedged at most US carriers. A move back above $100 crude pushes jet crack spreads up and directly compresses Q2 margins.
Same jet fuel cost passthrough as DAL; United has higher long-haul international exposure where fuel burn per ASM is greater.
Southwest historically hedges but recent disclosures show limited hedge coverage; near-pure domestic carrier with thin margins is most sensitive on a percentage basis.
Express air network and ground fleet make fuel a major variable cost; fuel surcharges lag spot prices by weeks, creating a margin squeeze on the way up.
Cruise lines burn marine bunker fuel, which tracks crude; Carnival has highest leverage and weakest balance sheet among cruise peers, amplifying the cost hit.

Dollar General's low-income customer is acutely sensitive to gasoline prices; sustained $100 crude historically correlates with traffic deceleration and trade-down to food staples away from discretionary mix.
Naphtha/ethane feedstock costs rise with crude; commodity chemicals producer with limited near-term ability to pass through input cost spikes given soft end-market demand.

headlines: Global oil futures top $100 again after U.S.-Iran peace talks canceled

macro_events.event_summary: Iran war is disrupting energy markets and pushing European consumers toward Chinese EVs as oil prices spike and ICE vehicle costs rise.
primary_drivers: Higher oil prices accelerating EV adoption in Europe
Chinese EV makers gaining European market share at expense of legacy automakers
Energy security premium boosting domestic and non-Middle East oil/gas producers

beneficiaries.rationale: US supermajor with diversified non-Iran production benefits from sustained Brent premium from Iran war; direct revenue uplift on every dollar of crude.

losers.rationale: Stellantis has heavy European ICE exposure (Peugeot, Fiat, Opel) and lagging EV lineup; squeezed by oil-driven demand shift and Chinese price competition in mass-market segment.
Tesla European share already eroding to BYD/MG; an accelerated European EV shift toward cheaper Chinese models pressures Tesla's Model 3/Y volumes more than it helps overall EV TAM.

headlines: How the Iran war is driving Europe toward Chinese EVs
